question: How do I join the Office Hours or live workshop stream if I’m a student?
answer_llm:
If you’re a student, you join live Office Hours/workshops via **YouTube Live**, not the Zoom room.

- The **Zoom link is only for instructors/presenters/TAs**
- The **YouTube video link** is posted in the **announcements channel on Telegram and Slack** before the session starts
- You can also watch on the **DataTalksClub YouTube Channel**
- Ask live questions through **Slido** (the link is pinned in the chat when the session is live)
- Don’t post questions in the chat, since they may get missed if the room is active
answer_orig:
The zoom link is only published to instructors/presenters/TAs.

Students participate via YouTube Live and submit questions to Slido (link is pinned in the chat when live). The video URL should be posted in the [announcements channel on Telegram and Slack](https://t.me/dezoomcamp) before it begins. You can also watch live on the DataTalksClub [YouTube Channel](https://www.youtube.com/c/DataTalksClub).

Don’t post questions in chat as they may be missed if the room is very active.

question: Where should I begin if I’m just starting LLM Zoomcamp, and what pages/docs should I check first?
answer_llm:
If you’re just starting LLM Zoomcamp, begin with these:

1. **LLM Zoomcamp docs**: https://datatalks.club/docs/courses/llm-zoomcamp/  
2. **General Zoomcamp logistics docs**: https://datatalks.club/docs/courses/zoomcamp-logistics/  
3. **LLM Zoomcamp GitHub repository**: https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp

For the weekly workflow, the usual order is:

- Watch the lesson videos
- Work through the lesson notebooks/code
- Read the homework instructions on GitHub
- Submit answers on the course platform before the deadline

Also, to track the syllabus, deadlines, homework, and progress, check the **course management platform**:  
https://courses.datatalks.club/llm-zoomcamp-2026/

If you want, I can also point you to the **Module 1 starting materials** specifically.

question: I don’t see my name on the leaderboard — how do I find my own entry?
answer_llm:
Click **“Jump to your record on the leaderboard”** — your account is automatically assigned a random display name, so that link will take you to your own entry.

If you want to check or change the name shown publicly, go to **“Edit Course Profile.”**
answer_orig:
When you set up your account, you are automatically assigned a random name, such as “Lucid Elbakyan.” Click on the "Jump to your record on the leaderboard" link to find your entry.

If you want to see what your Display name is, click on the "Edit Course Profile" button.

<{IMAGE:image_1}>

- **First field:** This is your nickname/displayed name. You can change it if you want to be known by your Slack username, GitHub username, or any other nickname of your choice. This is useful if you want to remain anonymous.
- **Second field:** Change this to your official name as in your identification documents—passport, national ID card, driver's license, etc. This is mandatory if you do not want "Lucid Elbakyan" on your certificate. This name will appear on your Certificate!

question: Can I do the course on my own schedule and still get a certificate, or is it only for people in the live cohort?
answer_llm: No. You can follow the course in self-paced mode, but you **won’t get a certificate** that way. Certificates are only awarded if you complete the course with a **live cohort**, because the certificate requires you to **peer-review 3 capstone projects**, which is only possible while the course is running.
answer_orig:
No, you can only get a certificate if you finish the course with a "live" cohort.

We don't award certificates for the self-paced mode. The reason is you need to peer-review 3 capstone(s) after submitting your project.

You can only peer-review projects at the time the course is running; after the form is closed and the peer-review list is compiled.

question: Why does the homework content keep changing before it’s released?
answer_llm:
If the homework title contains **`[DRAFT]`**, it means the homework is **not ready yet**. Until both of these are true:

- the homework form is open on the course management platform, and
- the title does **not** contain `[DRAFT]`,

the content can still change. Working on it early is at your own risk because the final version may be different.
answer_orig:
If the homework title contains `[DRAFT]`, it means the homework is not ready yet.

The homework is ready only when both are true:

- The homework form is open on the course management platform.
- The homework title does not contain `[DRAFT]`.

Until then, the content can still change. Working on the material or homework in advance is at your own risk, because the final version can be different.

question: Why do I get an OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits when I run responses.create?
answer_llm:
You get that error because your OpenAI API account has **insufficient credits / quota**. The `responses.create()` call is failing due to billing limits, not because of your code.

To fix it:
1. **Add credits** to your OpenAI account (pay-as-you-go, minimum $5).
2. Make sure you’re using a model available to your account in `responses.create(model=...)`.
3. You may need to **recreate your API key** after adding credits and update it locally.

If you still see a 429/quota-related error after that, it usually means the account’s billing/quota still isn’t enabled properly.
answer_orig:
You may receive the following error when running the OpenAI `responses.create` command due to insufficient credits in your OpenAI account:

```
OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits
```

question: What does it mean if responses.create fails with an insufficient credits error?
answer_llm: It means your OpenAI account has no available API credits/quota left. You’ll need to add credits or otherwise resolve your billing/quota issue before `responses.create` will work.
answer_orig:
You may receive the following error when running the OpenAI `responses.create` command due to insufficient credits in your OpenAI account:

```
OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits
```

question: Is the OpenAI responses.create command broken, or is the error from my account balance?
answer_llm:
It’s likely from your account balance/quota, not a broken `responses.create` command.

The context says `responses.create` can fail with:

- **`OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits`**
- **`RateLimitError: Error code: 429` / `insufficient_quota`**

question: Why am I getting a 429 RateLimitError from OpenAI when I try to call the API?
answer_llm:
You’re likely getting a **429 RateLimitError** because your OpenAI account has **insufficient quota/credits**, not because you’re simply sending too many requests.

This usually means:

- Your account has **no billing set up** or **no remaining credits**
- There is **no free usage** for the OpenAI API
- The error message may say `insufficient_quota`

### How to fix it
1. **Add credits** to your OpenAI account billing page  
   - Minimum is usually **$5**
2. Make sure you’re using a **model available to your account**
3. If needed, **recreate your API key** after adding credits and update it locally

If you’re getting regular 429s during the course even with billing set up, it can also be due to hitting per-minute request limits. In that case:
- use **retry with backoff**
- reduce concurrency
- cache results
- batch requests when possible

If the error specifically says **`insufficient_quota`**, it’s a billing/quota issue.

For more information on this error, read the docs: [https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/error-codes/api-errors.](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/error-codes/api-errors.)', 'type': 'insufficient_quota', 'param': None, 'code': 'insufficient_quota'}

question: Why does the course recommend uv instead of pip and virtualenv for Python setup?
answer_llm:
The course recommends `uv` because it’s **fast and convenient** and acts as an **all-in-one tool**.

It replaces several traditional tools at once, including:

- `pip`
- `virtualenv`
- `pip-tools`
- `pipx`
- `poetry`
- `pyenv`
- `twine`

So instead of managing Python versions, virtual environments, and dependencies separately, you can do it with one tool and get **reproducible lockfiles**. The lessons use it because it makes setup **simpler and faster**.

That said, you **don’t have to use it** — `pip` and `virtualenv` still work fine.

It's an all-in-one tool that replaces pip, virtualenv, pip-tools, pipx, poetry, pyenv, and twine — instead of juggling several tools, you manage Python versions, virtual environments, and dependencies through one fast command with reproducible lockfiles.

You're not required to use it — pip and virtualenv still work fine — but uv makes the setup simpler and faster, which is why the lessons use it.

Official docs: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/

question: How do I keep my OpenAI or other API keys in a .env file and load them in Python without hardcoding them?
answer_llm:
Store them in a `.env` file and load them with `python-dotenv`.

1. Add `.env` to `.gitignore`:
```gitignore
.env
```

2. Create a `.env` file:
```bash
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
GROQ_API_KEY=gsk_...
GEMINI_API_KEY=...
```

3. Install `python-dotenv`:
```bash
pip install python-dotenv
```

4. Load the variables in Python:
```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

openai_api_key = os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY")
groq_api_key = os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY")
```

This avoids hardcoding keys in your code.

question: What do I need to set up if I want to run Gemini in the Module 1 notebook using the OpenAI-style endpoint?
answer_llm:
You need three things:

1. A Gemini API key in your `.env` file, for example `GEMINI_API_KEY`.
2. An OpenAI client configured with Google’s OpenAI-compatible base URL.
3. The Gemini model name in your request.

Example:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)
```

Then call the model by name, e.g. `gemini-3.1-flash-lite`.

If you use the OpenAI-style endpoint, note that you’ll need to adapt notebook code that assumes the newer Responses API, especially for reading outputs and handling tools/function calls.
answer_orig:
To get started you need three things:

1. A Gemini API key saved in your `.env` file, for example as `GEMINI_API_KEY`.
2. An OpenAI client pointed at Google’s OpenAI-compatible base URL.
3. Your selected Google Gemini model name in your request.

Example code (loads the API key from `.env`, creates the Gemini client, and defines the `llm` helper):

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

def llm(instructions, user_prompt, model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite"):
    message_history = [
        {"role": "developer", "content": instructions},
        {"role": "user", "content": user_prompt}
    ]

    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=message_history
    )

    return response.choices[0].message.content
```

This uses the older chat completions style via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint, whereas many course examples use the newer Responses format. That means you will need to change the notebook code in a few places, especially where it reads the model response and where it handles tools or function calls.

question: Why does the notebook code need changes when I switch from Responses format to the older chat completions approach with Gemini?
answer_llm:
Because the two APIs return data in different shapes.

With the newer **Responses format**, the notebook code usually reads things like:

- `response.output`
- `response.output_text`
- Responses-style tool/function call handling

But when you switch to Gemini via the **older chat completions** interface, the response comes back in the chat-completions shape:

- `response.choices[0].message.content`

So the notebook needs changes anywhere it:

1. **reads the model output**
2. **handles tool/function calls**
3. **uses structured output parsing**

For Gemini through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint, you should use the chat-completions style, and adapt the code accordingly.

question: How do I get Pydantic-style structured output from Gemini when the OpenAI-compatible endpoint doesn't support responses.parse?
answer_llm:
Use the OpenAI SDK’s **chat-completions parsing flow** instead of `responses.parse`.

Define your schema as a Pydantic model, then call:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=YourModel
)
```

Then read the parsed object from:

```python
response.choices[0].message.parsed
```

Example:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from openai import OpenAI
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

class Question(BaseModel):
    question: str
    answer: str

class Questions(BaseModel):
    questions: list[Question]

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

messages = [
    {"role": "developer", "content": "Generate three FAQ questions about RAG."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Topic: structured output with Gemini"}
]

response = client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=Questions
)

result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
print(result.questions)
```

This works with Gemini through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint and gives you Pydantic-style structured output without manual JSON parsing.

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Question(BaseModel):
    question: str
    answer: str

class Questions(BaseModel):
    questions: list[Question]
```

Then call `chat.completions.parse` with `response_format=Questions`. Pass the same kind of `messages` you would to any chat completion:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

messages = [
    {"role": "developer", "content": "Generate three FAQ questions about RAG."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Topic: structured output with Gemini"}
]

response = client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=Questions
)

result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
print(result.questions)
```

Notes:
- This approach keeps using the OpenAI SDK while leveraging Gemini through Google's endpoint.
- The parsed output (e.g., `result.questions`) is a Pydantic object available directly, with no manual JSON parsing.
- `chat.completions.parse` requires `pydantic` (`uv add pydantic`).

question: How do I install Ollama on my machine, like on Mac, Windows, or Linux?
answer_llm:
Install Ollama from the official download page:

- Go to: https://ollama.com/download
- Choose your OS:
  - **macOS**: download the `.pkg` and install it
  - **Windows**: download the `.msi` and install it
  - **Linux**: run

```bash
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
```

After installation, you can test it by running:

```bash
ollama run llama3
```

This will download and start the model locally.

- **Windows**: Download the `.msi` and install it.
- **Linux**: Run the following command in the terminal:

  ```bash
  curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
  ```

Once installed, open a terminal and type:

```bash
ollama run llama3
```

This command will:

```bash
curl http://localhost:11434
```

You should receive a response similar to:


Then, install the Python client with:

```bash
pip install ollama
```

```python
import ollama

    model='llama3',
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": your_prompt}]
)

print(response['message']['content'])
```

question: Why does my `tiktoken` token count not match the number OpenAI shows in the API response?
answer_llm:
`tiktoken` only counts the visible text you pass in, but OpenAI’s API includes extra internal chat-format tokens.

Common reasons for the mismatch:

- Each chat message adds about 4 structural tokens
- The API adds 2 tokens to mark the start of assistant generation
- Extra whitespace, newlines, or uncommon Unicode can also change the count

So it’s normal if `tiktoken` says 320 while OpenAI reports something like 327.
answer_orig:
When using `tiktoken.encode()` to count tokens in your prompt, you might see a difference compared to OpenAI’s API response. For instance, you might get 320 tokens, while OpenAI reports 327. This is due to internal tokens added by OpenAI’s chat formatting.

Here’s what happens:

- Each message in a `chat.completions.create()` call (e.g., `{role: "user", content: "..."}`) adds 4 structural tokens (role, content, separators).
- The API adds 2 tokens globally to mark the start of assistant response generation.
- Extra newlines, whitespace, or uncommon Unicode characters in your content may slightly increase the token count.

Thus, even if your visible text is 320 tokens, OpenAI may count 327 due to these internal additions.

question: When I count tokens locally for a chat prompt, why is OpenAI reporting a few extra tokens?
answer_llm:
OpenAI may report a few extra tokens because of **internal chat formatting tokens** that aren’t part of your visible text.

Specifically:
- Each message in `chat.completions.create()` adds **4 structural tokens**.
- OpenAI also adds **2 global tokens** to signal the start of assistant generation.
- Extra whitespace, newlines, or uncommon Unicode characters can add a bit more.

So your local `tiktoken.encode()` count can be lower than what OpenAI reports.

question: How can I keep my OpenAI API key loaded automatically in a project folder instead of exporting it in every new terminal?
answer_llm:
Use **dirdotenv** so the key loads automatically when you enter the project folder:

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv
```

Add this to your `~/.bashrc` or `~/.zshrc`:

```bash
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh
```

Then, inside your project:

```bash
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, the API key is loaded automatically whenever you `cd` into that directory.

Also make sure `.env` and `.envrc` are in `.gitignore` so the key doesn’t get committed.
answer_orig:
Use [`dirdotenv`](https://github.com/alexeygrigorev/dirdotenv). It is like `direnv`, but works with both `.env` and `.envrc`, and is more portable across shells and operating systems.

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv

# add this line to your ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh

# inside your project:
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, the key is loaded automatically when you `cd` into the project directory.

Important: always add `.env` and `.envrc` to `.gitignore` so keys never land on GitHub.

`direnv` is also fine if you already use it. In that case, create `.envrc`, add your exports there, and run `direnv allow`.

For GitHub Codespaces, use the built-in [Codespaces secrets](https://docs.github.com/en/codespaces/managing-your-codespaces/managing-your-account-specific-secrets-for-github-codespaces) instead of files in the repo.

For Python scripts, the equivalent is `python-dotenv`:

```python
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()  # loads .env from project root
```

question: How do I figure out the best field weights in minsearch without guessing too much?
answer_llm:
The best way is to **evaluate different field weights against a small ground-truth set**, rather than guessing.

A simple approach:
1. Create a set of representative questions.
2. Mark which documents should be retrieved for each question.
3. Try different weight combinations in `minsearch`.
4. Compare retrieval metrics like **hit rate, precision@k, recall@k, or MRR**.

For small projects, trial and error can work, but **systematic evaluation is more reliable**.

question: What’s the best way to clean up docs before using them for RAG so retrieval works better?
answer_llm:
The best approach is to make the documents **clean, structured, and easy to chunk and retrieve**.

In practice:

- Remove obvious noise: broken HTML, duplicate text, boilerplate, OCR errors, repeated headers/footers.
- Preserve useful context: titles, section names, dates, page numbers, speaker names, Q&A structure.
- Store the result in a structured format if helpful, often **JSON**.
- Chunk the docs so related context stays together.
- Keep metadata for filtering or ranking later.

The goal is not just shorter text — it’s **better retrieval accuracy**.

question: I keep seeing "Failed to hardlink files" in GitHub Codespaces when using uv. Is that actually a problem, or can I ignore it?
answer_llm:
No — it’s not an error. In GitHub Codespaces, `uv` may not be able to hardlink files from its cache, so it shows that warning and falls back to copying the files instead. The package still installs.

If you want to suppress the warning, set:

```bash
export UV_LINK_MODE=copy
```

For a persistent change:

```bash
echo 'export UV_LINK_MODE=copy' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```
answer_orig:
No. This warning can happen in GitHub Codespaces when `uv` cannot hardlink files between the cache and the target environment.

The package still installs. `uv` falls back to copying files.

To suppress the warning for the current shell:

```bash
export UV_LINK_MODE=copy
```

To make it persistent:

```bash
echo 'export UV_LINK_MODE=copy' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```

See the `uv` documentation for more details: [https://docs.astral.sh/uv/](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/).

question: Why does WSL2 say the model needs more memory than available even though my PC has enough RAM?
answer_llm:
WSL2 is only allowed to use the amount of RAM assigned to it, not all the RAM in your PC. So even if your computer has enough memory, WSL2 may still report that the model needs more memory than is available because its WSL2 memory limit is lower.

To fix it, increase WSL2’s memory allocation with a `.wslconfig` file in:

`C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`

Example:

```ini
[wsl2]
memory=8GB
```

Then restart WSL:

```bash
wsl --shutdown
```

You can verify the new limit inside WSL with `free`.
answer_orig:
Your WSL2 is set to use Y.Y GiB, not all your computer memory. To allocate more RAM, follow these steps:

1. Create a `.wslconfig` file under your Windows user profile directory: `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`.

2. Include the desired RAM allocation in the file:

   ```ini
   [wsl2]
   memory=8GB
   ```

3. Restart WSL using the command:

   ```bash
   wsl --shutdown
   ```

4. Run the `free` command in WSL to verify the changes.

For more details, read [this article](https://www.aleksandrhovhannisyan.com/blog/limiting-memory-usage-in-wsl-2/).

question: Where should I put the .wslconfig file if I want to give WSL2 more memory?
answer_llm:
Put it in your Windows user profile directory:

`C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`

For example:

`C:\Users\alice\.wslconfig`

question: My GitHub Codespace is stuck on “Finishing up” and won’t reconnect — what’s the fastest thing I can try?
answer_llm:
The fastest thing to try is:

1. Go to [github.com/codespaces](https://github.com/codespaces)
2. Stop the codespace
3. Start it again

If it still says “Finishing up,” try opening it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code, or switch to another browser like **Edge, Chrome, or Brave**.
answer_orig:
These are usually GitHub Codespaces reliability issues rather than a problem with the course, so there's no single guaranteed fix — but the following workarounds resolve most cases.

**Codespace won't connect / stuck on "Finishing up":**

- Go to [github.com/codespaces](https://github.com/codespaces), stop the codespace, and start it again.
- If it still won't connect, open it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code, or try a different browser (Edge/Chrome/Brave).
- As a last resort, delete the codespace and create a new one.

**"My setup is all gone":**

- The repo in `/workspaces` persists across stop/start, but a **rebuild or a brand-new codespace** starts from a clean image, and system/global installs outside your project don't always survive. Reinstalling is quick with `uv` (`uv sync` / `uv add ...`).
- **Commit and push your work often** — uncommitted changes survive a stop/start but are lost if you delete or recreate the codespace.

If Codespaces keeps being flaky for you, consider running the course locally instead — see "[Can I run the course locally instead of Codespaces?](026_aa310de435_can-i-run-the-course-locally-instead-of-codespaces.md)".

question: Why did my setup disappear after I rebuilt or recreated the Codespace, and what survives across restarts?
answer_llm:
Your setup disappeared because a **rebuild** or a **brand-new Codespace** starts from a **clean image**. That means:

- **Project files in `/workspaces` persist across stop/start**
- **System/global installs outside your project may not survive** a rebuild or new codespace
- **Uncommitted changes survive stop/start, but are lost if you delete or recreate the codespace**

What survives across restarts:
- The repo in `/workspaces`
- Your uncommitted changes, as long as you only **stop/start** the codespace

What does **not** reliably survive:
- A rebuild
- Deleting/recreating the codespace
- System-wide or global installs outside the project

If needed, reinstalling with `uv` is usually quick (`uv sync` / `uv add ...`).

answer_llm:
You should **commit and push regularly**.

question: Can I use something other than OpenAI for the RAG exercises, or does the course require OpenAI specifically?
answer_llm:
No — the course does **not** require OpenAI specifically. You can use **any LLM provider** that works for you, including:

- OpenAI-compatible providers like **Groq, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Gemini, Z.ai, Mistral**
- Hosted open models via **Hugging Face**
- Local models with **Ollama, vLLM, LM Studio**
- Your own served model endpoint

If the provider is OpenAI-compatible, you usually only need to change the **`base_url`**, **API key**, and **model name**.
answer_orig:
If the provider used in the course isn't available or is blocked in your region (or you simply prefer another one), you can use any other LLM provider — the course isn't tied to OpenAI. Just switch to something else:

- Hosted, OpenAI-compatible providers — e.g. Groq, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Gemini, Z.ai, Mistral. The course code uses the OpenAI client, so you usually only need to change the `base_url`, the API key, and the model name.
- Open models via Hugging Face (e.g. Qwen, Llama) if you prefer hosted open-source models.
- Serve a model locally with [Ollama](https://ollama.com/), [vLLM](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm), LM Studio, or anything else — no external API call at all, so regional blocks don't apply and you don't need a paid key. Most of these also expose an OpenAI-compatible endpoint, so the course code works with only a `base_url` change.
- Rent a GPU machine and serve the model there (e.g. with vLLM) if your own machine can't run the model you want. This gives you a private OpenAI-compatible endpoint to point the course code at — just remember to stop/delete the instance when you're done so you don't keep paying for it.
- A VPN also works if you just need to reach a provider that's blocked at the network level.

Anything with an OpenAI-compatible endpoint (or a locally served model) will work — pick whatever is available and convenient for you.

For a curated list of options, see [Awesome LLMs](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/awesome-llms.md) in the course repo, which collects OpenAI API alternatives and tools for running models locally.

question: How can I keep from getting 429 rate limit errors in the RAG notebooks when I’m looping over lots of docs?
answer_llm:
To avoid 429 rate limit errors in the RAG notebooks, try to **reduce the number of API calls** rather than just handling errors afterward:

- **Use retries with backoff**: `openai` can retry automatically. For example:
  ```python
  from openai import OpenAI
  client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
  ```
- **Lower concurrency**: if you use threads/processes, keep it small, around **2–3 workers**.
- **Cache results**: save embeddings/LLM outputs to disk and reuse them on reruns.
- **Batch requests**: send multiple inputs in one API call when possible.
- **Use a cheaper/free provider**: `gpt-4o-mini` or Groq’s free tier can help.

If the error says **`insufficient_quota`**, that’s not a rate limit problem — it means your OpenAI billing/quota needs to be fixed.
answer_orig:
Free-tier and low-spend OpenAI accounts have per-minute and per-day request limits that are easy to blow through when a notebook loops over many documents. Reduce the number of API calls before you worry about handling the error after the fact.

- **Retry with backoff.** The `openai` Python client retries automatically with exponential backoff; raise the limit so transient 429s resolve themselves instead of crashing the run:

  ```python
  from openai import OpenAI

  client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
  ```

- **Lower concurrency.** In a thread/process pool, keep the pool small (2-3 workers) so you stay under the per-minute cap. A larger pool finishes one batch fast and then fails on the next.
- **Cache results.** Write embeddings and LLM responses to disk (JSONL, pickle, or a vector DB) and reload on re-run. Re-executing a notebook should not re-call the API for inputs you already processed.
- **Batch where possible.** Group independent inputs into a single request (e.g. embed a list of texts in one call) instead of looping one-by-one.
- **Use a cheaper/free provider.** `gpt-4o-mini` is cheap enough for the whole course; Groq's free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`) has generous per-minute limits via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

If you already see `insufficient_quota`, that is a billing issue, not a rate limit — see [OpenAI: Error: RateLimitError: Error code: 429](openai-error-ratelimiterror-error-code-429).

question: In agentic RAG, what exactly do people mean by a tool or function?
answer_llm:
In agentic RAG, a **tool** or **function** means an external capability the model can call when it needs help doing something it shouldn’t or can’t do by itself.

Examples include:

- searching documents
- querying a database
- calling an API
- running a calculation

In this course, **tools and functions are basically the same idea**—don’t worry too much about the naming difference.

The key point is that in **agentic RAG**, the **model decides when to call the tool/function**. In basic RAG, retrieval is usually a fixed step that happens before the model answers.

question: How is tool calling in agentic RAG different from the retrieval step in basic RAG?
answer_llm:
In **agentic RAG**, tool calling is **decided by the model during inference**: the model can choose to call a tool when it needs external help, like searching documents or querying a database.

In **basic RAG**, retrieval is usually a **fixed, predefined step** that happens **before** the model generates its answer.

So the key difference is:
- **Agentic RAG:** model decides **whether and when** to use a tool.
- **Basic RAG:** retrieval happens **automatically as part of the pipeline**.

question: Why do I get `AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'output'` when I call OpenAI’s Responses API with a model from another provider like Mistral or Groq?
answer_llm:
Because `client.responses.create(...)` and `response.output` are part of OpenAI’s **Responses API**, which is **OpenAI-specific**. Providers like **Mistral, Groq, Gemini, Anthropic**, etc. do not implement that API shape, so the object you get back is usually just a string or a chat-completions-style response, not something with an `.output` attribute.

For non-OpenAI providers, use the **chat completions** API instead and read:

```python
response.choices[0].message.content
```

Example:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,  # may need adapting per provider
)
return response.choices[0].message.content
```

So the error happens because you're mixing an **OpenAI-only response accessor** with a **different provider’s response format**.
answer_orig:
The new OpenAI Responses API (`client.responses.create(...)`, accessed via `response.output`) is OpenAI-specific. Other providers (Mistral, Groq, Gemini, etc.) don't implement it.

For non-OpenAI providers, use the chat-completions API and read `response.choices[0].message.content`:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,  # may need adapting per provider
)
return response.choices[0].message.content
```

You'll also have to adapt the tools schema to whatever shape your provider expects.

It is okay to use the older `chat.completions` API for homework or projects if your provider supports that interface better than the Responses API.

question: Why am I getting Error code 400 about missing tools[0].function in Azure OpenAI, and how should I fix the weather tool JSON?

Why do I get a 404 error with toyaikit when I switch from OpenAI to Groq or another provider that isn't OpenAI?
answer_llm:
You get the 404 because `toyaikit`’s `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` use OpenAI’s **Responses API** (`responses.create`), and that endpoint is **only implemented by OpenAI**.

When you switch to Groq or another non-OpenAI provider, they may not support `responses.create`, so you get a 404.

Use the **chat completions** classes instead, which call the standard `chat.completions.create` endpoint that Groq and other OpenAI-compatible providers support:

- `OpenAIChatCompletionsClient`
- `OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner`

If you want Anthropic, use:

- `AnthropicClient`
- `AnthropicMessagesRunner`

If you want, I can show the exact code change for Groq or Anthropic.
answer_orig:
If you get a `404` (or similar) when using toyaikit with Groq or another non-OpenAI provider, it's because `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` call OpenAI's Responses endpoint (`responses.create`), which only OpenAI implements. Switch to the **chat completions** classes, which use the standard `chat.completions.create` endpoint that Groq and other OpenAI-compatible providers support.

### OpenAI or Groq (chat completions)

```python
import os
from openai import OpenAI
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIChatCompletionsClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)   # functions need type hints + an Args: docstring

# OpenAI:
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="gpt-4o-mini", client=OpenAI())

# Groq (same runner, just point the OpenAI client at Groq's base URL):
groq_client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://api.groq.com/openai/v1",
)
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile", client=groq_client)

runner = OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

### Anthropic

```python
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import AnthropicClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import AnthropicMessagesRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)

# Reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from the environment automatically:
llm_client = AnthropicClient(model="claude-haiku-4-5")

runner = AnthropicMessagesRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

Notes:

- `runner.loop(prompt=...)` runs one turn programmatically and returns a result with `.last_message`, `.all_messages`, and `.tokens` — no chat interface needed. Use `runner.run()` only for the interactive Jupyter loop.
- Pass an explicit, current model id. For Anthropic, `claude-haiku-4-5` / `claude-sonnet-4-5` work; an outdated id can 404.
- For Groq you'll see a harmless `UnknownModelWarning: No pricing data...` — the call still succeeds, only cost calculation is skipped.

question: Why am I getting a 402 error from OpenRouter when I call responses.create(), and what should I change in my code?
answer_llm:
You’re getting a **402 from OpenRouter** because `responses.create()` is being called **without a reasonable `max_output_tokens` limit**. OpenRouter checks the **maximum possible output size** up front, and with no limit it may assume something very large, which can cause a **402 payment/quota rejection** before the model runs.

### What to change
Add `max_output_tokens` to your `responses.create()` call:

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

### If you’re using `rag_helper.py`
Add the same parameter in the `llm()` method.

### If you’re using ToyAIKit
Pass it via `extra_kwargs`:

```python
llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

### Also check
Make sure your `.env` points to OpenRouter correctly:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

If it still fails, check your OpenRouter key credits/limits.
answer_orig:
OpenRouter can return APIStatusError with code 402 when responses.create() is called without a reasonable max_output_tokens limit. This happens because OpenRouter bills/limits checks against the maximum possible output (which can be very large, around 65536 tokens), so a free or low-limit key can be rejected before the model runs. This is different from a direct OpenAI endpoint (which typically returns 429 for insufficient quota).

Fix

Pass a lower limit in your responses.create() call:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI()  # uses OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENAI_BASE_URL from .env

response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework with rag_helper.py, add the same parameter in the ``llm()`` method.

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework Q6 with ToyAIKit:

```python
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIClient

llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

1024 is enough for homework answers; you can raise it later if needed.

If it still fails

1) Confirm your `.env` points at OpenRouter:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

2) Check your OpenRouter key limit and remaining credits at https://openrouter.ai/settings/keys

3) Prefer a pinned model (for example `openai/gpt-oss-120b:free`) instead of `openrouter/free`, which can route to models with different limits.

Note: This behavior is different from OpenAI’s typical 429 handling for insufficient quota. If you still encounter issues after these steps, double-check the model and endpoint configuration to ensure the key is valid and has sufficient credits.

- you’re using a pinned model like `openai/gpt-oss-120b:free` rather than `openrouter/free`

If it still gives 402 after setting `max_output_tokens`, check the key limits and remaining credits at OpenRouter settings.
answer_orig:
OpenRouter can return APIStatusError with code 402 when responses.create() is called without a reasonable max_output_tokens limit. This happens because OpenRouter bills/limits checks against the maximum possible output (which can be very large, around 65536 tokens), so a free or low-limit key can be rejected before the model runs. This is different from a direct OpenAI endpoint (which typically returns 429 for insufficient quota).

Fix

Pass a lower limit in your responses.create() call:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI()  # uses OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENAI_BASE_URL from .env

response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework with rag_helper.py, add the same parameter in the ``llm()`` method.

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework Q6 with ToyAIKit:

```python
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIClient

llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

1024 is enough for homework answers; you can raise it later if needed.

If it still fails

1) Confirm your `.env` points at OpenRouter:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

2) Check your OpenRouter key limit and remaining credits at https://openrouter.ai/settings/keys

3) Prefer a pinned model (for example `openai/gpt-oss-120b:free`) instead of `openrouter/free`, which can route to models with different limits.

Note: This behavior is different from OpenAI’s typical 429 handling for insufficient quota. If you still encounter issues after these steps, double-check the model and endpoint configuration to ensure the key is valid and has sufficient credits.

question: If I use the full documents in the index instead of chunks, what breaks in the RAG flow?
answer_llm:
Using the full documents instead of chunks breaks the RAG retrieval flow because the index is supposed to be built on the **chunked documents**, not the original full documents.

What goes wrong:
- The retriever no longer returns **small relevant chunks**.
- The prompt can end up with **too much context**.
- You lose the main RAG benefit: **retrieving only the most relevant pieces** before sending them to the model.

So the correct flow is:

```python
index.fit(chunks)
```

not:

```python
index.fit(documents)
```

If you use full documents, the retrieval step is no longer chunk-based, and the context reduction part of RAG breaks.

question: For the Module 1 homework, how should I count tokens if I'm using a non-OpenAI model provider?
answer_llm:
For a non-OpenAI provider, get the token count from the **model response object** and use that provider’s **usage fields**.

- Check fields like `response.usage.input_tokens` or `response.usage.prompt_tokens`, depending on the API style.
- Do **not** use `tiktoken` or `cl100k_base` as a generic tokenizer for Gemini, Mistral, Hugging Face, Groq, etc., because tokenization differs by model.
- If the provider does not expose token usage, use that provider’s **native tokenizer** as an approximation.
- For multiple-choice homework questions, choose the **closest option**.

question: Why is my homework link getting rejected with a 500 or other non-200 status, and what should I check first?
answer_llm:
The checker expects your submitted URL to return HTTP `200` when it does a GET request.

If you get `500` or another non-200 status, the first things to check are:

- **Is the repo public?** Private repos usually can’t be reached by the checker.
- **Did you include `.git`?** Submit the plain URL, like `https://github.com/you/repo`, not `https://github.com/you/repo.git`.
- **Is the URL typed exactly right?** Check for typos.

A quick first test: open the link in a private/incognito browser window. If it loads there, it should work for the checker too.
answer_orig:
The submission checker fetches the URL you submit with a GET request and expects an HTTP `200` response. A non-200 status (404, 500, etc.) almost always means the link isn't publicly reachable as-is. Check the usual causes:

- **Private repo** — make the repository public so the checker can access it.
- **Trailing `.git`** — submit the plain repository URL (e.g. `https://github.com/you/repo`), not `https://github.com/you/repo.git`.
- **Typo in the URL** — paste the exact link.

A quick test: open the URL in a private/incognito browser window. If it loads for you there, it'll work for the checker too.

question: What exactly are embeddings in vector search, and why do we need them?
answer_llm:
Embeddings are a way to convert non-numerical data, like text, into numbers while preserving meaning and context.

Why we need them:
- Computers can do math on numbers, not directly on words.
- If two pieces of text are similar in meaning, their embeddings should be close to each other in vector space.
- That lets us use mathematical methods like dot product or cosine similarity to find semantically similar items in vector search.

In short: embeddings turn meaning into vectors so we can compare, search, and rank data mathematically.
answer_orig: Embeddings refer to the process of converting non-numerical data into numerical data while preserving meaning and context. When similar non-numerical data is input into an embedding algorithm, it should yield similar numerical data. The proximity of these numerical values allows for the use of mathematical semantic similarity algorithms. Related concepts include the "vector space model" and "dimensionality reduction."

question: How do embeddings turn text or other non-numeric data into numbers without losing meaning?
answer_llm:
Embeddings convert text or other non-numeric data into numbers in a way that preserves meaning and context.

The key idea is:

- similar inputs produce similar numeric vectors
- the distance or proximity between those vectors reflects semantic similarity
- this lets you compare meaning using math

So instead of turning text into arbitrary numbers, embeddings place it in a vector space where related items end up close together.
answer_orig: Embeddings refer to the process of converting non-numerical data into numerical data while preserving meaning and context. question: Why should similar inputs end up with similar vectors in an embedding model?

question: What are the vector space model and dimensionality reduction, and how are they related to embeddings?
answer_llm:
The **vector space model** is a way of representing text or other data as **vectors** in a mathematical space, so that similar items are close together. This makes it possible to compare them using math, like dot product or cosine similarity.

**Dimensionality reduction** is the process of reducing the number of dimensions in that vector representation while trying to preserve the important information or meaning.

question: Why did the lesson use a plain dot product for similarity instead of computing cosine similarity explicitly?
answer_llm:
The lesson used a plain dot product because the embeddings were assumed to already be normalized to unit length. For unit vectors, the dot product is exactly the same as cosine similarity, so there was no need to compute cosine explicitly.

In the homework, the embeddings are not guaranteed to be normalized by default, so you need to normalize them first if you want dot product to equal cosine similarity.
answer_orig:
In the lesson, `.dot(...)` was used under the assumption that the embeddings returned by the model (e.g., `model.encode(...)` from OpenAI) are already normalized to have unit length. In that case, the dot product is mathematically equivalent to cosine similarity.

In the homework, however, we use classic embeddings like TF-IDF + SVD, which are not normalized by default. This means that the dot product does not represent cosine similarity unless we manually normalize the vectors.

question: For vector search in this module, do we embed just the question, just the answer, or both together?
answer_llm:
There’s no single right answer — it’s an experiment on your dataset.

The course shows three options:

- **Embed the answer only** (`text`)
- **Embed the question only**
- **Embed both together**: `question + " " + text`

The module says to pick whichever gives the best **hit rate / MRR** on your ground-truth set. Often, **question + text** works best, but it’s a bit longer and slightly more expensive.

question: How does cosine similarity measure how close two vectors are in text search?
answer_llm: Cosine similarity measures how close two vectors are by looking at the angle between them, not their length. In text search, a value closer to 1 means the vectors point in nearly the same direction, so the documents are more similar in content. If the vectors are unit-normalized, cosine similarity is just their dot product.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity is a measure used to calculate the similarity between two non-zero vectors, often used in text analysis to determine how similar two documents are based on their content. This metric computes the cosine of the angle between two vectors, which are typically word counts or TF-IDF values of the documents. The cosine similarity value ranges from -1 to 1, where 1 indicates that the vectors are identical, 0 indicates that the vectors are orthogonal (no similarity), and -1 represents completely opposite vectors.

question: Why is cosine similarity useful when comparing documents by their word counts or TF-IDF values?
answer_llm:
Cosine similarity is useful because it measures how *similar the direction* of two document vectors is, regardless of their length.

For word counts or TF-IDF values, that means it compares the overall pattern of terms in two documents, rather than just favoring longer documents with larger counts. So two documents with similar content will have a high cosine similarity even if one is much longer than the other.

question: What does a cosine similarity score of 1, 0, or -1 actually mean?
answer_llm:
A cosine similarity score of:

- **1** means the vectors are **identical** in direction.
- **0** means the vectors are **orthogonal**, so they have **no similarity**.
- **-1** means the vectors are **completely opposite**.

question: Can cosine similarity be used only for non-zero vectors, and why is that important?
answer_llm:
Yes — cosine similarity is used for **non-zero vectors**.

- Cosine similarity is based on the **angle** between vectors.
- A **zero vector** has no direction, so its angle with another vector is undefined.
- That’s why cosine similarity only makes sense for vectors with non-zero length.

question: In vector search, how do you interpret the angle between two document vectors using cosine similarity?
answer_llm:
The angle tells you how aligned the document vectors are:

- **Small angle** → vectors are very similar
- **90° angle** → vectors are unrelated / orthogonal
- **180° angle** → vectors point in opposite directions

With **cosine similarity**, you use the cosine of that angle:
- **1** means identical direction
- **0** means no similarity
- **-1** means opposite direction

question: Why does cosine similarity become just a dot product after normalizing vectors?
answer_llm:
Because normalization makes each vector have unit length.

Cosine similarity is:

\[
\cos(\theta) = \frac{x \cdot y}{\|x\|\|y\|}
\]

If both vectors are normalized, then \(\|x\| = 1\) and \(\|y\| = 1\), so:

\[
\cos(\theta) = x \cdot y
\]

So after normalization, cosine similarity is just the dot product.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity measures how aligned two vectors are, regardless of their magnitude. When all vectors (including the query) are normalized to unit length, their magnitudes no longer matter. In this case, cosine similarity is equivalent to simply taking the dot product between the query and each document embedding. This allows us to compute similarities efficiently using matrix multiplication.

question: How does unit-length normalization make the vector sizes irrelevant in cosine similarity?
answer_llm:
Unit-length normalization makes vector sizes irrelevant because every vector is scaled to have length 1.  

That means cosine similarity depends only on the angle between vectors, not their magnitude. So once all vectors are normalized:

- the length no longer affects the result,
- cosine similarity becomes the same as the dot product,
- and you can compare vectors efficiently with matrix multiplication.

If vectors are not normalized, their sizes would affect the dot product, so it would not equal cosine similarity.

question: In vector search, why can we use matrix multiplication to compare a query with all embeddings?
answer_llm: We can use matrix multiplication because, once the embeddings and the query are normalized, cosine similarity becomes just a dot product. Then a single matrix-vector multiplication computes the similarity between the query and all embeddings efficiently at once.

question: What’s the connection between cosine similarity and multiplying the embedding matrix by the query vector?
answer_llm:
The connection is that if the embeddings and the query vector are **normalized to unit length**, then **cosine similarity becomes the same as the dot product**.

So multiplying the embedding matrix by the query vector:

- computes the dot product between the query and every embedding
- which is equivalent to cosine similarity when vectors are normalized
- and matrix multiplication lets you do this efficiently for all documents at once

So, in short: **embedding matrix × query vector = fast cosine similarity search, assuming normalization**.

question: Why is cosine similarity between normalized vectors so much faster to compute with matrix operations?
answer_llm:
Because normalized vectors turn cosine similarity into a plain dot product, and dot products can be computed very efficiently as a single matrix multiplication.

So instead of comparing the query to each vector one by one in Python loops, you can stack all embeddings into a matrix and do one optimized linear algebra operation. That uses fast low-level BLAS/GPU routines and avoids Python-loop overhead, which is why it’s much faster.

question: Why do the dot-product and the for-loop version of vector search return slightly different numbers?
answer_llm:
They can differ slightly because of floating-point precision.

The dot-product version uses optimized matrix/BLAS operations, while the for-loop adds numbers sequentially. The order of addition is different, and floating-point addition is not perfectly associative, so the last few decimal places can change.

The results are effectively the same. Use a tolerance check like:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```
answer_orig:
In [Lesson 4 (Vector Search)](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/02-vector-search/lessons/04-vector-search.md) we compute the scores two ways — `scores = X.dot(v_query)` and the equivalent for-loop — and they can come out slightly different. This is normal, it's just floating-point precision. If you compare them with `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` you might get `False`, even though both compute the same dot products. They just add the numbers up in a slightly different order, and floating-point addition isn't perfectly associative, so the results can differ in the last few decimal places.

Under the hood, `X.dot(v)` runs optimized BLAS code that sums in a different order than a sequential Python loop. The math is the same; only the rounding differs, so the values aren't really "wrong."

Just compare them with a small tolerance instead of expecting an exact match:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```

question: I got `False` from `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` even though both methods should be the same — is that a bug?
answer_llm:
No, it’s not a bug. `X.dot(v_query)` and the loop compute the same dot products, but they may accumulate floating-point rounding errors in a different order. That can make `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` return `False` with the default tolerance.

Use a small tolerance, for example:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```

So the values are effectively the same; they just differ in the last decimal places.

question: Why am I getting IndexError: list index out of range when I try to access the best chunk?
answer_llm:
This usually happens when your embeddings and chunks are misaligned — for example, you generated fewer embeddings than there are chunks, so the “best chunk” index points past the end of the list.

Make sure you create embeddings directly from the chunk list:

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

`X` should have the same number of rows as `len(chunks)`. If they don’t match, indexing into `chunks[best_idx]` can raise `IndexError: list index out of range`.
answer_orig:
The error typically happens when the number of embeddings you generate does not match the number of document chunks. Make sure you create embeddings directly from the chunk list:

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

The number of rows in `X` should be equal to `len(chunks)`.

The best chunk lookup fails with a list out of range error when the number of embeddings does not match the number of document chunks.

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
```

question: How do I fix the mismatch between embeddings and chunks so I don’t get an IndexError?
answer_llm:
Make sure the number of embeddings matches the number of chunks by creating embeddings directly from the chunk list, not from the original documents.

For example:

```python

X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

Then `X` should have the same number of rows as `len(chunks)`. This prevents the `IndexError` when you access the best chunk.

question: Why should the number of embeddings be the same as the number of document chunks?
answer_llm:
Because each document chunk should have exactly one embedding. Embeddings are created from the chunk list, so the number of rows in the embedding matrix should match `len(chunks)`.

If they don’t match, you can get errors like `IndexError: list index out of range` when you try to access the best chunk. The correct approach is to generate embeddings directly from the chunks:

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

So: one chunk → one embedding.

question: For Module 2 Q2, my cosine similarity is way off and not in the multiple-choice list — could I be using the wrong embedding model?
answer_llm:
Yes — the most common issue is using the wrong embedding model.

For Module 2 Homework Q2, you should **not** use `sentence-transformers`. Use the lightweight ONNX `Embedder` from `embedder.py` instead:

```python
from embedder import Embedder
model = Embedder()
```

If you use a different model like `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` or `all-mpnet-base-v2`, your cosine similarity can end up outside the multiple-choice options.

Also double-check that you are:

- embedding the page’s **`content`** field
- comparing it with the **query vector from Q1**
- using normalized vectors, so the **dot product equals cosine similarity**

If the value is still slightly off after that, choose the closest option.
answer_orig:
The most common cause is using a different embedding model than the homework specifies. Homework 2 tells you **not** to use `sentence-transformers` and to use the lightweight ONNX `Embedder` from `embedder.py` instead:

```python

model = Embedder()
```

Both approaches produce the same vectors for the same model, but if you embed with a different model (for example the `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` model from the lessons, or `all-mpnet-base-v2`) you'll get a cosine value that isn't among the options.

Also check that:

- You're embedding the page's `content` field (not the filename or the whole dict).
- You're comparing against the query vector from Q1.
- The vectors are normalized — the `Embedder` returns normalized vectors, so the dot product is the cosine similarity directly.

If your value is still slightly off after using the right model, pick the closest option — small numerical differences are expected.

question: How do I set up Gemini, OpenAI, and Tavily keys for Kestra so the module can read them?
answer_llm:
Set them as environment variables **before starting Kestra**. Kestra reads secrets from vars prefixed with `SECRET_`, and those values must be **base64-encoded**.

Use:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"                              # used by AI Copilot
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n $GEMINI_API_KEY | base64)             # used by the flows
export SECRET_OPENAI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-openai-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for flow 3
export SECRET_TAVILY_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-tavily-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for web search (flows 3, 5, 6)
```

Then restart Kestra:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

In a flow, reference the secret **without** the `SECRET_` prefix:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

Don’t commit the keys to Git.
answer_orig:
Kestra reads secrets from environment variables that are prefixed with `SECRET_` and whose value is **base64-encoded**. You export them in the terminal *before* starting Kestra. For Gemini you need two variables — the plain one (used by the AI Copilot) and the base64-encoded `SECRET_` one (used by the flows):

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"                              # used by AI Copilot
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n $GEMINI_API_KEY | base64)             # used by the flows
export SECRET_OPENAI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-openai-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for flow 3
export SECRET_TAVILY_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-tavily-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for web search (flows 3, 5, 6)
```

Then start (or restart) Kestra so it picks up the variables:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

Inside a flow, reference a secret **without** the `SECRET_` prefix:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

See the [setup lesson](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/03-setup.md) for the full walkthrough. Never commit your keys to Git.

question: Why does Kestra AI Copilot keep saying it can only help with creating Kestra flows, and what am I missing in the setup?
answer_llm:
Kestra AI Copilot is saying that because it likely **isn’t receiving a valid Gemini API key**, so it falls back to a default refusal message.

What you’re probably missing is this:

- It expects the **plain** environment variable: `GEMINI_API_KEY`
- It does **not** use `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` for the Copilot

Fix:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"

```

- You exported it in the **same shell** where you run `docker compose up`
- You can verify it with:

```bash

```

If it still doesn’t work, the key may be:

- missing or mistyped
- rate-limited / quota exhausted
- outdated, so try generating a fresh one in Google AI Studio

Important: if you also use the flows, you may need **both**:

- `GEMINI_API_KEY` for the Copilot
- `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` for the flows

question: Why does Kestra say my Gemini API key is missing or invalid even though I already set SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY?
answer_llm:
Kestra is likely not seeing the secret because `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` is being created incorrectly or Kestra wasn’t restarted after setting it.

Most common issue: the base64 value is corrupted by a trailing newline or space. Use `echo -n` and quote the variable:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Also check these:

- Restart Kestra after exporting the variable:
  ```bash
  docker compose up -d
  ```
- In your flow, reference it without the `SECRET_` prefix:
  ```yaml
  {{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
  ```
- Make sure you export it in the same shell session you use to start Kestra.

A cleaner option is to use a `.env` file and start Kestra with:

```bash
docker compose --env-file ./.env -f ./docker-compose.yml up -d
```

If you want, I can also help you verify your exact shell commands.
answer_orig:
The most common cause is a corrupted base64 value. If you encode the key with a trailing space or newline, the decoded secret is wrong and authentication fails.

Use `echo -n` (which omits the trailing newline) and quote the value:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Other things to check:

- You must **restart** Kestra after exporting, so the container picks up the new variables: `docker compose up -d` (or `docker compose down && docker compose up -d`).
- Reference the secret in flows **without** the `SECRET_` prefix: `{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}`.
- Export the variables in the same shell session you start Kestra from.

A cleaner alternative is to put the keys in a `.env` file and pass it explicitly:

```bash
docker compose --env-file ./.env -f ./docker-compose.yml up -d
```

This avoids adding an extra newline or accidental whitespace.

question: Why should I use an orchestrator like Kestra instead of just running everything in a notebook?
answer_llm:
Use Kestra when you need more than “run this notebook once.”

Notebooks are great for learning and experimenting, but real workflows usually need:

- scheduling
- retries
- monitoring
- secret management
- reliable chaining of multiple tasks

Kestra provides that orchestration layer.

In this course, Kestra is also used because it supports the AI workflows being taught:
- AI Copilot to generate flows from natural language
- RAG to ground responses in real data
- AI agents that decide which tools to call

So the point is not just to run code — it’s to see how AI fits into production-style workflows, not only notebook cells.

If you want, I can also explain when a notebook is enough and when an orchestrator becomes worth it.
answer_orig:
Notebooks are great for learning and experimenting, but real AI workflows need more than a script that runs once: scheduling, retries, monitoring, secret management, and reliably chaining tasks together. That's what an orchestrator like Kestra provides.

In this module Kestra is also the vehicle for the AI techniques the course is teaching: AI Copilot to generate flows from natural language, RAG to ground responses in real data, and AI agents that decide which tools to call. The goal is to see how AI fits into production-style workflows, not just notebook cells.

Kestra's AI plugins also work with any major provider (OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, and more), so you can swap providers in a flow without changing anything else. See the [module intro](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/01-intro.md) for the full motivation.

question: If I build a flow with OpenAI and later want to switch to Gemini or Anthropic, do I have to rewrite the whole thing?
answer_llm:
No — you usually do **not** have to rewrite the whole thing.

If your flow is built around an **OpenAI-compatible endpoint**, switching to **Gemini**, **Groq**, **DeepSeek**, etc. often just means changing:

- the **base URL**
- the **API key**
- the **model name**

For **Anthropic**, you may need to switch to the **Anthropic-specific client/runner** if your code uses OpenAI’s Responses endpoint, since that endpoint is OpenAI-only. But the overall flow logic and tools usually stay the same.

For Gemini specifically, the course notes say you can use Google’s OpenAI-compatible endpoint, but you may need to adjust some notebook code where it reads responses or handles tools/function calls.

question: Can I use Groq or Ollama with Kestra, or is it Gemini-only?
answer_llm:
Yes — you can use **Groq or Ollama with Kestra**. It’s **not Gemini-only**.

Kestra’s AI plugin is **provider-agnostic**, so it supports:
- **OpenAI**
- **Gemini**
- **Anthropic**
- **xAI / Grok**
- any **OpenAI-compatible provider**
- local models via **Ollama** or **LM Studio**

For Groq specifically, it works because Groq is **OpenAI-compatible**.  
For Ollama, you can use a local model served through Ollama.

The course uses **Gemini** mainly because of its generous free tier, but you can swap providers in the flow without changing the rest of the setup.
answer_orig:
Kestra's AI plugin is provider-agnostic: it supports OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, xAI, Grok, and any OpenAI-compatible provider, including local models served through Ollama or LM Studio. You swap the provider block in a flow without changing anything else. See the [full list of supported providers](https://kestra.io/plugins/plugin-ai/provider).

The course uses Gemini because it has a generous free tier, but you are free to use any provider. The [awesome-llms list](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/awesome-llms.md) in the course repo tracks free and free-tier options; Groq is a popular choice because it is OpenAI-compatible and works with both the chat completions and responses APIs.

There are two ways to use AI in Kestra:

- The **AI plugin** (`io.kestra.plugin.ai`) is the generic one. It is the most flexible for switching providers, though new vendor-specific API features take a bit longer to land here.
- The **provider-specific plugins** (e.g. `plugin-gemini`, `plugin-openai`) expose features unique to that vendor, such as Gemini video generation, before they reach the generic AI plugin.

For OpenAI-compatible providers that don't have their own plugin (DeepSeek, Groq, xAI), point the OpenAI plugin at the provider's base URL instead of the default endpoint.

question: Can I build a Kestra flow without typing YAML or writing code at all?
answer_llm:
Yes. You can build a Kestra flow without hand-writing YAML or writing code by using:

- the **no-code form editor**
- the **AI Copilot**
- **external agent skills** like Claude Code, Codex, or Kestra CTL

So the short answer is: **yes, you can do it with no hand-written YAML or code**.
answer_orig:
Yes. You have three options that require no hand-written YAML or code:

- **The no-code form editor** builds a flow from a form instead of YAML. It is form-based (not drag-and-drop like n8n), so it suits people who don't want to edit YAML directly.
- **The AI Copilot** generates a flow from a natural-language description, and you can iterate with follow-up messages to add tasks or change the order. See the [AI Copilot lesson](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/04-ai-copilot.md).
- **External agent skills** (Claude Code, Codex, Kestra CTL) let you generate flows and push them into Kestra from your editor without touching the Kestra UI.

Unlike Airflow, where business logic and orchestration logic are intertwined in Python, Kestra keeps the two separate: your Python script stays a plain script, and the workflow YAML just describes how and when to run it. This is closer to a cron job than to a traditional Airflow DAG.

For simple point-to-point automations (e.g. "post a Slack message when a YouTube video goes live"), a tool like Zapier or n8n may be simpler and cheaper. Kestra's advantage is observability, retries, and handling more complex or technical workflows in one place.

question: How does the AI Copilot in Kestra help if I want to create a flow from plain English?
answer_llm:
Kestra’s AI Copilot can take a plain-English description and generate a Kestra flow YAML for you. You can then refine it with follow-up messages, like adding tasks or changing the order.

If it only says it can assist with creating Kestra flows, it usually means Kestra didn’t get a valid **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY`. In that case, set the key and restart Kestra:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
docker compose up -d
```

Also note: the Copilot uses `GEMINI_API_KEY`, while the flows use `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`.

question: Why does ChatGPT sometimes invent Kestra plugin fields like bucket or name in flow YAML?
answer_llm:
Because a generic AI like ChatGPT isn’t grounded in the Kestra plugin schema for your exact Kestra version, so it may guess plausible field names that don’t actually exist.

For example, it may invent `bucket` and `name` for a GCS upload task, when the real field is `to`:

```yaml
type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.gcs.Upload"
to: "gs://my-bucket/path/to/file"
```

This can also happen with BigQuery, where it may split the destination into `projectId`, `dataset`, and `table`, but the real field is `destinationTable`.

How to avoid it:
- Check the generated YAML against the official Kestra plugin docs
- Use Kestra’s built-in AI Copilot, which is grounded in the schema of your running version
- Validate/test the YAML in your Kestra environment

Plugin properties can also vary by version, so always verify against your installed Kestra release.
answer_orig:
A general AI (e.g., ChatGPT) isn't grounded in Kestra's plugin schemas for your running version, so it can surface plausible but invalid property names (such as `bucket`/`name`) instead of the real, version-specific property. For example:

**GCS Upload** — invented `bucket`/`name` props vs. the real `to` property:

```yaml
# Incorrect (invented props)
- id: upload_to_gcs
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.gcs.Upload"
  bucket: "my-bucket"
  name: "path/to/file"

# Correct
- id: upload_to_gcs
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.gcs.Upload"
  to: "gs://my-bucket/path/to/file"
```

**BigQuery LoadFromGcs** — split `projectId`/`dataset`/`table` vs. the real `destinationTable`:

```yaml
# Incorrect (pseudo-split properties)
- id: load_to_bq
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.bigquery.LoadFromGcs"
  projectId: "my-project"
  dataset: "my_dataset"
  table: "my_table"

# Correct
- id: load_to_bq
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.bigquery.LoadFromGcs"
  destinationTable: "my-project.my_dataset.my_table"
```

To avoid this:

- Cross-check generated YAML against the official plugin docs: https://kestra.io/plugins/plugin-gcp
- Use Kestra's built-in AI Copilot, which is grounded in the current plugin schema for your running version.
- Validate and test your YAML in your Kestra environment to ensure it parses and runs as expected.

Note that plugin properties can vary by plugin version; what's correct in one release may be invalid in another. If you share a snippet, we can help verify it against the docs.

question: How do I know the YAML generated for Kestra is using the real plugin properties for my version?
answer_llm:
Check the generated YAML against the **official Kestra plugin docs for your exact version**. Plugin properties can change between releases, so what looks valid from an AI-generated flow may not actually exist in your installed version.

question: Why do we bother checking token usage in Kestra flows if the model is already running fine?
answer_llm:
Because “running fine” doesn’t tell you how much it’s costing.

In Kestra flows, token usage matters because:

- **LLM providers bill by tokens** — tracking usage maps directly to your spend.
- **Flows can run on schedules or in loops** — costs can add up quickly even if everything succeeds.
- **You can optimize prompts** — token counts help you shorten prompts and responses without losing quality.
- **You can add guardrails** — set thresholds/alerts so a runaway flow doesn’t quietly create a big bill.

So token tracking is mainly about **cost control and safety**, not just whether the model is technically working.
answer_orig:
Token usage is tracked because it maps directly to what LLM providers charge you for, and Kestra workflows run LLM calls on a schedule or in loops where costs add up fast. The main reasons:

- **Cost control**: tokens are the unit providers bill by, so tracking usage lets you budget, forecast, and spot cost spikes before they get expensive.
- **Prompt optimization**: seeing how many tokens each prompt and response consumes helps you tighten prompts and trim output without losing quality.
- **Guardrails**: in long-running or looping flows, usage monitoring lets you set thresholds and alerts so a runaway task doesn't quietly rack up a large bill.

In practice, each AI task in a Kestra flow can capture usage from the provider's response (for example `usage.total_tokens`), and you can surface those numbers in logs, metrics, or dashboards to keep an eye on spend per run.

question: How does tracking tokens help with keeping LLM costs under control in scheduled or looping workflows?

Tracking tokens helps because tokens are what LLM providers bill for. In scheduled or looping workflows, many LLM calls can run repeatedly, so even small token counts can add up quickly.

- **Estimate and control cost** per run

- **Set thresholds/alerts** to stop runaway loops
- **Optimize prompts** to use fewer tokens

In Kestra, you can capture usage from the LLM response, like `usage.total_tokens`, and use that in logs, metrics, or dashboards.

question: Why does Docker Desktop say mounts denied when I try to run Kestra with a path under /tmp, and what should I change in the settings?
answer_llm:
Docker Desktop says **“mounts denied”** because it’s not allowed to share the host path Kestra is trying to mount, usually something under **`/tmp`** like `/tmp/kestra-wd`.

You should change Docker Desktop settings here:

- Open **Docker Desktop**
- Go to **Settings**
- Open **Resources > File Sharing**
- Add **`/tmp`**  
  or the exact folder, like **`/tmp/kestra-wd`**
- Click **Apply & restart**

After Docker restarts, run Kestra again.
answer_orig:
This error means Docker Desktop isn't allowed to access the host path the container is trying to mount (commonly `/tmp/kestra-wd` when running Kestra). Add the path to Docker Desktop's file sharing list:

1. Open Docker Desktop.
2. Click the gear icon (Settings) in the top right.
3. Go to **Resources > File Sharing**.
4. Click the **+** (Add) button.
5. Enter `/tmp` (or the specific path like `/tmp/kestra-wd`) and press Enter.
6. Click **Apply & restart**.

Once Docker restarts, rerun `docker compose up -d` to start Kestra.

question: For the Module 3 Kestra homework, what am I supposed to put in the public repo link if I did everything in the Kestra UI and not in normal code files?
answer_llm:
Put the **flow YAML files** you created in Kestra into the public repo and link that repo.

Even if you did everything in the Kestra UI, the homework expects you to:

- **download/export the flows** you made or edited
- **commit them to a public GitHub repo** (or other code-hosting site)
- include any **scripts** those flows call, if applicable
- optionally add a short **README** summary

A common structure is:

- `module-3/flows/your-flow.yaml`

Then paste the **repo link** into the submission form. The form answers themselves go in the form fields, separately.
answer_orig:
The submission form asks for a link to a public repo (GitHub or any code-hosting site), but unlike earlier modules the work happens inside the Kestra UI rather than as code you write from scratch. That leaves people unsure what to put in the repo.

Download the Kestra flows you created or edited for the homework and commit them to the repo. The flow YAML files are the code you submit.
